md: ### When data is too big to list one by one

When you have 30 students' marks you can list them and find the mean by adding and dividing. When you have **30,000** students' marks, you can't. So you **group** the data into intervals (called **classes**), record the **frequency** in each, and compute summary statistics from the table.
Each row is a **class** (interval). The numbers tell us how many students scored in each range — not their exact scores. The challenge: compute mean, median, and mode from this summary.

blocks.title: The three measures we'll compute
blocks.body:
**Mean** ($\bar{x}$) — the 'average', total marks / number of students.

**Median** — the middle value when data is ordered.

**Mode** — the most frequently occurring value (or class).

For grouped data, each measure has its own formula because we don't know individual values.

md: Before the formulas, build the picture. The histogram widget below takes raw data and shows the frequency in each bin — exactly the structure of the table above. Slide **bins** higher or lower and watch what happens to the **modal class** (highlighted red) and the **mean from class marks**:

Every class has a **class mark** $x_i$ = midpoint of the interval (e.g., $0$–$10$ has class mark $5$). We assume every observation in that class equals its class mark — a reasonable approximation when intervals aren't too wide.

blocks.title: Method 1: Direct method
blocks.body:
$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$

where $x_i$ is the class mark and $f_i$ is the frequency.

**Procedure:** for each row, multiply $f_i \cdot x_i$. Sum these products. Divide by total frequency.

Use this when numbers are small. Gets tedious when class marks are large.

blocks.title: Method 2: Assumed-mean method
blocks.body:
Pick a convenient class mark $a$ (the 'assumed mean'). Compute deviations $d_i = x_i - a$.

$\bar{x} = a + \dfrac{\sum f_i d_i}{\sum f_i}$

The $d_i$ are smaller numbers — easier arithmetic. Pick $a$ near the middle of the data.

blocks.title: Method 3: Step-deviation method
blocks.body:
If all classes have equal width $h$, define $u_i = (x_i - a)/h$. Then

$\bar{x} = a + h \cdot \dfrac{\sum f_i u_i}{\sum f_i}$.

Now $u_i$ are small integers — much easier arithmetic. This is the fastest method when class widths are equal (which is the typical setup).

md: Marks data from the table above. Class width $h = 10$. Pick assumed mean $a = 35$ (class mark of $30$–$40$, the modal-looking class).
**1. Build a cumulative frequency (cf) column** — running total of $f_i$.
**3. Identify the median class** — the first class whose $cf$ reaches or exceeds $N/2$.
$cf$ = cumulative frequency **of the class BEFORE the median class**
Notice: $cf$ here is the cf of the *preceding* class, not the median class itself. Get this right or your answer will be off.
**Modal class** = class with the highest frequency.

blocks.title: Mode worked example
blocks.body:
Modal class for our table is **30–40** (highest frequency, $f_1 = 51$).

$f_0 = 23$ (frequency of 20–30), $f_2 = 6$ (frequency of 40–50), $l = 30$, $h = 10$.

$\text{Mode} = 30 + \dfrac{51 - 23}{2(51) - 23 - 6} \cdot 10$

$= 30 + \dfrac{28}{73} \cdot 10 \approx 30 + 3.84 = 33.84$ marks.

md: RHS = $3(31.96) - 2(29.8) = 95.88 - 59.6 = 36.28$. Computed mode was 33.84. Close — empirical means *approximately*, not exact.

blocks.title: Ogive — the cumulative-frequency curve
blocks.body:
An **ogive** is a graph of cumulative frequency vs. upper class boundary (less-than ogive) or vs. lower class boundary (more-than ogive).

The **less-than ogive** plots points $(\text{upper limit}, \text{cf})$ and joins them with a smooth curve. It rises from 0 on the left to $N$ on the right.

**To find median graphically:** find the y-value $N/2$ on the less-than ogive, drop a perpendicular to the x-axis. That x-value is the median.

When both ogives are drawn together, they intersect at the median.
